finding: The `review-budget-exhausted`/`audit-budget-exhausted` blocker fires when "recorded rounds reached review_max_rounds" (┬¦4.6) and ┬¦12.5 pins it as "a recorded review round count equal to review_max_rounds". But `review_max_rounds`/`audit_max_rounds` default to 0 meaning "no cap" (unchanged by this release), and the spec never carves out cap=0. At a fresh project in the `review` phase, recorded rounds = 0 equals cap = 0, so a literal implementation fires the escalate blocker on the very first `tp resume`, halting the driver before any round runs. Two engineers test this contradictorily: one treats 0 as no-cap (blocker never fires), the other implements count==cap literally (fires at 0==0).

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: State explicitly that a cap of 0 means no cap and the budget-exhausted blocker never fires when the effective `review_max_rounds`/`audit_max_rounds` is 0; require cap>0 AND recorded==cap. Add a ┬¦12.5 assertion that with the default cap=0 no budget blocker is emitted in any phase.

finding: The implement payload carries task.acceptance (┬¦4.4.3), but next_action.command for implement is `tp next` "which claims and returns the full task" ŌĆö so acceptance is re-emitted verbatim by the very next call the driver makes. Unlike review/audit payloads (round/unresolved_findings are cheap ints that pre-stage a fan-out the single command does NOT re-return), implement's preview duplicates tp next's imminent output. Worse, ┬¦4.2 classifies payload as machine-actionable, so acceptance (potentially multi-line) survives --compact; yet no machine step in the reference driver (┬¦9.2) reads it: the null-task case is already signaled by the no-ready-task blocker, resume-vs-claim by `wip`, and the actual acceptance text is consumed only after tp next runs. This recurs on every resume in `implement`, the most frequent phase.

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: Drop `acceptance` from the implement payload (tp next supplies it one call later), keeping at most `id`+`wip` for driver logging/reconciliation routing ŌĆö or make the implement payload `{}` symmetric with decompose/release, since next_action.command already carries the work. Either way --compact then strips text the agent does not need at resume time (checklist #13).

finding: The review-budget-exhausted blocker (┬¦4.6 table, restated ┬¦12.5) is triggered solely by "recorded review rounds reached review_max_rounds without convergence" with no phase restriction. But ┬¦4.3 deliberately keeps a project in `implement` once tasks exist even when review reads unconverged, and ┬¦4.7/┬¦4.3 explicitly support a user `tp import --force` that decomposes past an exhausted, unconverged review budget. In that supported state the phase is `implement` (rule 1) yet the recorded review rounds still equal `review_max_rounds` and review is still unconverged, so `review-budget-exhausted` (an `escalate` blocker) would fire on every `tp resume` throughout the `implement` (and later `audit`) phase, halting the reference driver (┬¦9.2) on each unit. This contradicts ┬¦4.3's stated intent that an in-progress project stays in `implement` and proceeds, surfacing only `spec-stale` for a mid-implementation spec edit ŌĆö the design assumes implement is not re-blocked by review state. (audit-budget-exhausted is naturally phase-safe because audit rounds are only recorded after all tasks are done.)

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: Phase-gate the budget blockers: compute `review-budget-exhausted` only while phase is `review` and `audit-budget-exhausted` only while phase is `audit` (i.e. only for the active phase's sequence), or state explicitly in ┬¦4.6 that these blockers are emitted only in their corresponding phase so a force-imported project is not perpetually escalated during `implement`/`audit`.

finding: ┬¦5.3 promises that "when the effective strategy is `hc` and `hc` is not on `PATH`, tp prints a one-line stderr courtesy warning on strategy-reading commands". ┬¦12 never pins this warning, and its scope is ambiguous: the strategy-reading commands (┬¦5.2: `tp commit`, `tp done`, `tp config`) include both commands that are *rejected* under hc (does the exit-2 rejection ALSO carry the courtesy warning?) and commands that *succeed* (does a successful `tp done --commit <sha>` close still print the courtesy warning at exit 0?). Two engineers implement/test different command sets and different rejection-vs-warning interleavings.

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: Enumerate exactly which commands emit the ┬¦5.3 courtesy warning when effective=hc and hc is absent (including whether a rejected command emits both the exit-2 hint and the warning, and whether a successful `tp done --commit` close emits it), and add a ┬¦12 assertion pinning presence/absence per command.

finding: Under effective `hc`, ┬¦5.4 says a `tp done --batch` row lacking both `commit_shas` and `covered_by` "is rejected (exit 2) and the batch fails per its existing partial-failure semantics", and ┬¦12.9 says such a row "is rejected with exit 2". This conflates a row-level rejection with the process exit code: existing `tp done --batch` partial-failure semantics report closed/failed counts, and it is unpinned whether the whole command exits 2 when one row is invalid but other rows close successfully, or exits 0 (or some other code) with the bad row reported in a failed array. Two engineers write different assertions on the process exit code for a mixed batch.

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: Pin the process exit code of `tp done --batch` when >=1 row is invalid under hc but others succeed (e.g., command exits 0 and reports the row in `failed`, or exits with the batch's existing non-zero partial-failure code), separately from the per-row rejection reason. Update ┬¦12.9 to assert both the failed-row count and the command exit code for a mixed batch.

finding: ┬¦5.4 ("Closing a task under each strategy") frames itself as the complete, single rule and enumerates every close path under effective hc ŌĆö tp commit, tp done --auto-commit, tp done --commit, tp done --covered-by, tp done --batch, and bare tp done (rejected exit 2) ŌĆö but never mentions the low-level `tp close <id> "reason"` command. tp close today records no commit and accepts neither --commit nor --covered-by, yet ┬¦8.3 explicitly treats it as a first-class close path (it emits the post-close unexplained-changes warning). So under effective hc, bare `tp close` would close a task with no durable commit ŌĆö the exact state bare `tp done` is rejected to prevent (┬¦3.1 checkpoint invariant) ŌĆö giving an unaddressed bypass. The implementer must invent whether bare tp close is rejected under hc, allowed as an unchanged escape hatch, and how a covered-by close would even be expressed through it (tp close has no --covered-by flag).

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: In ┬¦5.4.2 (effective hc), state tp close's behavior explicitly: either reject a bare `tp close <id> "reason"` with exit 2 and the same hc hint (mirroring bare tp done) ŌĆö and then specify how/whether a covered-by close is possible via tp close ŌĆö or declare tp close an intentionally-unchanged low-level escape hatch that closes without a recorded commit (accepting the ┬¦3.1 incomplete-checkpoint trace). Do not leave it unenumerated.

finding: ┬¦6.2 asserts that under `builtin`, `tp commit` and `tp done --auto-commit` "make exactly one commit and record a single-element `commit_shas`" ŌĆö a new behavior (pre-0.28.0 these wrote only `commit_sha`). But ┬¦12 never pins the value of `commit_shas` produced by the builtin commit path: ┬¦12.9's builtin case only asserts "commit and close", and ┬¦12.10 exercises `commit_shas` exclusively through `tp done --commit`. An implementation where `tp commit`/`--auto-commit` writes only `commit_sha` (leaving `commit_shas` empty) violates ┬¦6.2 yet passes every ┬¦12 test, because a reader falling back to `commit_sha` (┬¦6.1) can't distinguish the two.

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: Add a ┬¦12 assertion: after `tp commit <id>` (and `tp done <id> --auto-commit`) under builtin, `commit_shas` is a single-element array equal to `[commit_sha]`.

finding: ┬¦7.3 defines keep-list matching by Go `filepath.Match` semantics, and ┬¦7.2 does not require `tp keep` to validate the glob on write ŌĆö so a malformed pattern (e.g. `[abc` with an unclosed character class) can be stored. `filepath.Match` returns `ErrBadPattern` for such a pattern, but neither ┬¦7.3 nor ┬¦4.5/┬¦4.8 pins how `tp resume` (the read-only oracle the whole driver depends on) behaves on a match error: abort with a non-zero exit, skip the bad entry, or treat it as a non-match sending the file to `changes`. Two engineers test the malformed-glob path differently.

resolved.evidence: Round-4 pins: budget-exhausted blocker excludes max_rounds=0 (no-cap) + phase-gated (review-budget only in review phase, audit-budget only in audit); implement payload drops redundant task.acceptance (tp next returns it); batch partial-failure exit clarified; low-level tp close follows per-strategy rule; tp keep validates glob (ErrBadPattern exit 2); ┬¦12 tests added for hc-absent courtesy warning, builtin single-element commit_shas, malformed-glob.

suggestion: Either require `tp keep` to reject a pattern that fails `filepath.Match` validation at write time (exit 2), or state that `tp resume` treats an entry whose pattern errors as a non-match (file -> `changes`) and never aborts. Add a ┬¦12 assertion for a stored malformed glob.
